rxn_class_summary: Palladium-catalyzed electrooxidative hydrofluorination of aryl-substituted alkenes (styrenes and α,β-unsaturated carbonyls) with a nucleophilic fluoride source (Et3N·3HF) and a hydrosilane to give benzylic fluorides
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of an η3-benzylpalladium(II) intermediate to a high-valent Pd(III) or Pd(IV) species, which enables C-F bond formation with nucleophilic fluoride; cathode reduces protons to H2 as the counter-reaction

key_intermediates.name: η3-benzylpalladium(II) species (II) formed from alkene insertion with hydrosilane
high-valent benzylpalladium(III/IV) intermediate (III) generated by anodic oxidation
benzyl carbocation (IV) from heterolysis of high-valent Pd-benzyl, stabilized by HFIP

electron_flow: Pd(II) inserts into the aryl alkene with hydrosilane to form an η3-benzyl-Pd(II); the anode oxidizes this complex by one or two electrons to a high-valent Pd(III/IV)-benzyl, which undergoes C-F bond formation with fluoride (either via reductive elimination or via a benzyl carbocation trapped by F−), regenerating Pd(II); protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.
rate_determining_step: Anodic oxidation of the η3-benzylpalladium(II) intermediate to the high-valent Pd species (the C-F forming step requires potentials above the onset potential of complex 41)

probe_reagents.name: stoichiometric η3-benzyl Pd(II)-(R)-Tol-BINAP complex 41
probe_reagents.observation: No fluorinated product without electrolysis or at E < Eonset; product 15 formed in 28±5% as a racemate at Ucell = 2.7 V (E > Eonset), supporting that anodic oxidation of the Pd(II)-benzyl is required

probe_reagents.name: chemical oxidants Ag(I), Cu(II), benzoquinone, Oxone, CAN, iodine(III)
probe_reagents.observation: None gave stoichiometric fluorination of 41, indicating the high-valent Pd intermediate is only accessible via anodic oxidation

probe_reagents.name: HFIP cosolvent
probe_reagents.observation: Racemic product from enantiopure Pd complex 41 plus HFIP stabilization argues for a benzyl carbocation pathway from the high-valent Pd

notes: Two C-F bond forming scenarios are proposed and cannot be distinguished: (favored) heterolysis of high-valent Pd-benzyl to a benzyl carbocation (IV) that is trapped by fluoride, consistent with the racemic product from enantiopure 41 and the role of HFIP; alternatively inner- or outer-sphere C-F reductive elimination directly from the high-valent Pd(III/IV) intermediate. A low-valent Pd(II)/Pd(0) pathway (pathway II) is ruled out because no product forms without applied potential.
